lessons.title: Lesson 1 — What logs actually are
lessons.intro: Before you can hunt, you need to know what you're hunting through. Take five minutes with me — I promise this is the part most courses skip.

sections.heading: A log is just a row in a spreadsheet
sections.body:
Every time something happens on a computer — someone signs in, a file opens, a network connection is made — the system writes a row to a list. That row is a 'log event'. It has a timestamp and a bunch of fields (columns) describing what happened. Imagine a giant Excel sheet where each row is one event. That's all a log is.

In Microsoft Sentinel, those rows live in **tables**. Different kinds of events go in different tables, the same way you'd have separate tabs in Excel for 'Sales' and 'Returns' and 'Customers'.

sections.heading: The tables you'll meet today
sections.body:
- **SigninLogs** — every time someone signs in to Azure AD / Office 365.
- **DeviceProcessEvents** — every time a program starts on a computer protected by Defender for Endpoint.
- **DeviceNetworkEvents** — every network connection made by those same computers.
- **OfficeActivity** — actions in Office 365 (file edits, sharing, mailbox rules).
- **AzureActivity** — changes made to the Azure cloud itself (someone creates a VM, changes a firewall rule).
- **AzureFirewallNetworkRule** — traffic going through the cloud firewall.
- **SysmonEvent** — deep Windows telemetry from a tool called Sysmon.
- **ThreatIntelligenceIndicator** — a list of known-bad IPs, domains, and file hashes.

sections.heading: A query is a question
sections.body:
When you ask 'show me all sign-ins from the IP 45.83.193.150 in the last hour', you're writing a question in a language called **KQL** (Kusto Query Language).

KQL is read left-to-right, top-to-bottom, like a pipeline. You start with a table, then add filters and transforms one at a time, separated by the pipe character `|`.

```kql
SigninLogs
| where IPAddress == "45.83.193.150"
| take 10
```

Read that out loud: 'Take the SigninLogs table, keep only rows where IPAddress is that one, then show me the first 10.'

exercise.prompt: Write a query that returns the first 5 rows of the DeviceProcessEvents table. No filters yet — just get something on screen.
exercise.hint: Start with the table name on its own line. Then add a pipe and 'take 5'.

lessons.title: Lesson 2 — The four operators you'll use every shift
lessons.intro: These four operators handle about 80% of all real SOC work. Master these and you can answer most questions.

sections.heading: 1) where — keep only the rows you care about
sections.body:
`where` filters rows by a condition. Read it as 'keep rows where this is true'.

```kql
SigninLogs
| where ResultType == "0"
```
Keeps only successful sign-ins (in Azure AD, ResultType '0' means success — yes, weird).

You can stack them:
```kql
SigninLogs
| where ResultType == "0"
| where IPAddress != "10.4.2.18"
```
Keeps successful sign-ins that are NOT from that one printer.

sections.heading: 2) project — pick which columns to show
sections.body:
Logs have dozens of columns. Most you don't care about. `project` keeps only the ones you want.

```kql
SigninLogs
| where ResultType == "0"
| project TimeGenerated, UserPrincipalName, IPAddress, City
```
Now you only see four columns instead of twenty. Easier to read.

sections.heading: 3) summarize — count and group
sections.body:
`summarize` is the magic one. It collapses many rows into a summary.

```kql
SigninLogs
| where ResultType != "0"
| summarize FailedCount = count() by IPAddress
```
Read it: 'Take failed sign-ins, then group them by IP address, and tell me how many fails per IP.' One row per IP, with the count.

You can summarize multiple things at once:
```kql
SigninLogs
| summarize Attempts = count(), Users = dcount(UserPrincipalName) by IPAddress
```
`dcount` means 'distinct count' — how many different users, not how many events. That's how you spot a password spray: one IP, many users.

sections.heading: 4) sort by — order the results
sections.body:
Self-explanatory.
```kql
SigninLogs
| where ResultType != "0"
| summarize Failures = count() by IPAddress
| sort by Failures desc
```
The worst offender goes to the top. `desc` = descending. Default is ascending if you don't say.

exercise.prompt: Find the top 5 IP addresses by number of failed sign-in attempts in SigninLogs. Show IP and failure count, biggest first.
exercise.hint: where ResultType != "0" gets the failures. Then summarize ... by IPAddress. Then sort by your count column desc. Then take 5.

lessons.title: Lesson 3 — Pivoting: building a timeline across tables
lessons.intro: A real incident touches multiple tables. The skill is taking a value you found in one table — an IP, a username, a host — and finding it again in another table to see the full story.

sections.heading: The pivot pattern
sections.body:
You find something suspicious in one table. You take the entity (the IP, user, host) and search for it in another table.

Example: Sign-in alert tells you `j.harrington@contoso.com` did something weird at 07:42. You now pivot to DeviceProcessEvents to see what their machine was doing around the same time.

Step 1 — find the host the user is on. Usually you'd check a CMDB; in this lab assume j.harrington uses WS-FIN-014.

Step 2 — pull every process on that host in a tight time window around the alert:
```kql
DeviceProcessEvents
| where DeviceName == "WS-FIN-014"
| where Timestamp between (datetime(2026-05-13T09:00:00Z) .. datetime(2026-05-13T11:00:00Z))
| project Timestamp, FileName, ProcessCommandLine, InitiatingProcessFileName
| sort by Timestamp asc
```
You'll see WINWORD launching, then PowerShell, then certutil, then rundll32. That's the attack chain in order.

sections.heading: Time windows with ago()
sections.body:
Instead of typing exact timestamps, use `ago()`:
```kql
DeviceProcessEvents
| where DeviceName == "WS-FIN-014"
| where Timestamp > ago(6h)
```
Means: from six hours ago to now. The lab's 'now' is frozen at 2026-05-13T12:00:00Z.

sections.heading: Cross-table joins (the heavier hammer)
sections.body: You can also join tables explicitly, but in practice you do most pivoting by copy-pasting an entity from one query into the next. That's what Tier 1 actually does on shift.

exercise.prompt: You just saw an alert about j.harrington from IP 185.220.101.42. Pivot to DeviceNetworkEvents and find every connection in the last 4 hours where RemoteIP == "185.220.101.42". Show timestamp, device, bytes sent, and the process that made the connection.
exercise.hint: Start with the table, filter on RemoteIP, filter on Timestamp > ago(4h), then project the columns you want.

lessons.title: Lesson 4 — Hunting patterns (the stuff that actually catches attackers)
lessons.intro: Once you've got the basics, these patterns let you catch the stuff that vanilla alerts miss. Memorise the shapes — they'll come up again and again.

sections.heading: Pattern: password spray (one IP, many users)
sections.body:
```kql
SigninLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(24h)
| where ResultType != "0"
| summarize Failures=count(), Users=dcount(UserPrincipalName) by IPAddress
| where Users > 20
| sort by Users desc
```
Flags any IP that failed against 20+ different users. That ratio is the giveaway.

sections.heading: Pattern: brute force (many tries, one user)
sections.body:
```kql
SigninLogs
| where TimeGenerated > ago(1h)
| where ResultType != "0"
| summarize Failures=count() by UserPrincipalName
| where Failures > 30
```
One account being hammered. Opposite shape from spray.

sections.heading: Pattern: beaconing (regular outbound to one IP)
sections.body:
```kql
DeviceNetworkEvents
| where Timestamp > ago(12h)
| summarize Connections=count() by DeviceName, RemoteIP, bin(Timestamp, 5m)
| summarize Buckets=count() by DeviceName, RemoteIP
| where Buckets > 10
```
Beaconing shows up as 'this host talked to this IP in lots of separate 5-minute buckets'. Real users don't do that — automation does.

sections.heading: Pattern: anomalous parent-child process
sections.body:
```kql
DeviceProcessEvents
| where Timestamp > ago(24h)
| where InitiatingProcessFileName in~ ("WINWORD.EXE", "EXCEL.EXE", "POWERPNT.EXE")
| where FileName in~ ("powershell.exe", "cmd.exe", "wscript.exe", "cscript.exe")
```
Office spawning a shell is almost always phishing. This one-liner finds patient zero.

sections.heading: Pattern: mass action by one user (deletion, sharing, etc.)
sections.body:
```kql
OfficeActivity
| where TimeGenerated > ago(1h)
| where Operation == "FileDeleted"
| summarize Count=count() by UserId, bin(TimeGenerated, 5m)
| where Count > 100
```
Humans don't delete 100 files in 5 minutes. Scripts do.

exercise.prompt: Use the beaconing pattern to find any (DeviceName, RemoteIP) pair that has communicated in more than 10 separate 5-minute buckets in the last 12 hours.
exercise.hint: Two-stage summarize: first by (DeviceName, RemoteIP, bin), then by (DeviceName, RemoteIP) counting the buckets.
